progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an interesting and potentially useful idea—improving adversarial robustness by fusing object- and scene/context-oriented representations—but the current presentation raises substantial concerns about clarity, rigor, and positioning. The biological motivation is engaging but overextended and sometimes speculative, and the method is not yet clearly distinguished from standard ensemble, feature-fusion, or context-bias approaches; in particular, calling the approach “multimodal” is somewhat misleading if both streams are visual CNN features from the same image rather than genuinely different modalities. The introduction would benefit from a sharper statement of the technical contribution, the exact fusion mechanism, and why it should improve robustness beyond ensembling or exploiting dataset/context correlations. The adversarial robustness framing also needs much more precision: the paper should define the threat model, attack settings, white-box vs. black-box assumptions, whether attacks are run through the full fused model, and how it avoids common pitfalls such as gradient masking or robustness from non-differentiable preprocessing. Claims that existing defenses “mostly” fail or that biological fusion confers adversarial stability are too broad without careful qualification. The writing contains numerous grammatical, formatting, and citation issues—e.g., “robust ness,” missing spaces before citations, encoding errors, inconsistent terminology—which reduce confidence in the paper’s polish. Overall, the core hypothesis is promising, especially if the experiments show clean-accuracy-preserving gains under strong adaptive attacks, but the paper needs a clearer methodological exposition, a more disciplined related-work comparison, and substantially more rigorous adversarial evaluation to support its main claims.

comments.title: Gaussian blur is treated as an adversarial attack without an adversarial construction
comments.quote: We tested the benefits of the fusion approach on preserving adversarial robustness for human perceivable (e.g., Gaussian blur) and network perceivable (e.g., gradient-based) attacks for CIFAR-10 and MS COCO data sets.
comments.explanation: Gaussian blur is a meaningful robustness or corruption test, especially when applied to foreground regions to probe whether contextual features help. However, the described procedure applies a predetermined blur rather than selecting blur location, strength, or parameters adversarially to maximize loss under a specified constraint. In standard adversarial-robustness terminology, this is closer to a common corruption or distribution shift than an adversarial attack in the same sense as FGSM/PGD. The paper should qualify the terminology so readers do not interpret the blur results as demonstrating adversarial robustness under a true adversarial threat model.

comments.title: Central “multimodal” framing is stronger than the actual visual feature-fusion setup
comments.quote: We propose that this biologically inspired approach to integrate information across multiple modalities provides a new way to improve adversarial robustness that can be complementary to current state of the art approaches.
comments.explanation: The biological motivation discusses integration across sensory modalities such as vision, audition, and olfaction, but the implemented system fuses two visual streams derived from the same RGB image: an ImageNet-pretrained object-oriented representation and a Places365-pretrained scene/background-oriented representation. This is a legitimate multi-stream or multi-feature visual fusion method, but it is not multimodal integration in the usual machine-learning sense. The robustness gain may come from different pretrained visual biases rather than from integration across genuinely different sensory modalities.

comments.title: Adversarial-defense literature is characterized too broadly
comments.quote: While many methods exist for detecting and defending against adversarial attacks, they do not generalise across a range of attacks and negatively affect performance on clean, unperturbed data. ... recent research has shown that these methods are mostly ineffective or their effectiveness is inconclusive ... adversarial training with multi step attack ... was initially proposed in Madry et al. (2017).
comments.explanation: The paper is right that many proposed defenses are attack-specific, that clean/robust accuracy tradeoffs are common, and that weak defense evaluations have often failed under stronger attacks. However, the wording is too categorical. It suggests that existing defenses generally fail to generalize and necessarily hurt clean accuracy, and that training-phase robustness methods as a class are mostly ineffective. PGD-style adversarial training, for example, is widely accepted as effective within specified norm-bounded white-box threat models, even though it has limitations and tradeoffs. The historical statement about Madry et al. is also too strong: Madry et al. popularized and formalized the robust-optimization/PGD adversarial-training framework, but adversarial training and iterative attack-based evaluation had predecessors. The literature framing should be narrowed to the specific limitations and threat models being discussed.

comments.title: Fine-tuning protocol is internally inconsistent for CIFAR-10
comments.quote: Only the 512 dimensional fully connected layer was finetuned for MS COCO or CIFAR-10. ... For the foreground classifier, we trained all layers of a Resnet18 on CIFAR-10, with weights initialized from a Resnet 18 pretrained on Imagenet.
comments.explanation: The methodology first states that only the final fully connected layer was fine-tuned for both MS COCO and CIFAR-10, implying frozen pretrained feature extractors. Later, however, the CIFAR-10 foreground classifier is described as being trained in all layers from ImageNet initialization, while the background classifier receives different treatment. This is a substantive contradiction because frozen features versus full fine-tuning affects clean accuracy, adversarial vulnerability, feature distributions, and the fairness of comparisons between foreground, background, and joint models. The MS COCO and CIFAR-10 training protocols should be separated and stated explicitly.

comments.title: MS COCO dataset size is numerically misstated
comments.quote: MS COCO dataset is a large dataset with 1.6 million images that have multiple objects and multiple instances with overlapping contexts.
comments.explanation: Standard MS COCO contains on the order of a few hundred thousand images, not 1.6 million images. A number around 1.5 million is closer to the number of labeled object instances or annotations. This is likely a wording error, but as written it misstates the scale of the source dataset and could mislead readers.

comments.title: COCO subset construction is underspecified and internally ambiguous
comments.quote: We pruned the MS COCO dataset for images with fewer than two instances (<=2) of a single object so as to minimise cases of co-occurring context. ... ‘Dissimilar’ and ‘Similar’ are eight classes randomly selected from the 24 classes such that the supercategories are either distinct or the same.
comments.explanation: The COCO filtering and subset construction are difficult to reproduce. The phrase “fewer than two instances” would normally mean 0 or 1 instance, whereas “<=2” means at most two instances. It is also unclear whether “of a single object” means one object instance, one category, or images containing only one annotated category possibly with multiple instances. Separately, the Similar/Dissimilar analysis appears to depend on randomly selected class subsets, but the classes, random seed, and whether results were averaged over multiple draws are not reported. Because object size, context regularity, category difficulty, and dataset bias vary substantially across COCO classes, these ambiguities affect the interpretation of the claimed context-variability effects.

comments.title: Foreground blur procedure is underspecified and does not guarantee intact context
comments.quote: In our approach, only the foreground pixels are modified leaving the context of the images intact. ... Gaussian blur was applied to the test set by convolving a portion of the image within the object bounding box given in the dataset with Gaussian kernel. ... The blur was applied to the bounding box area of the image, where main object was located.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is clear: corrupt the object region while preserving contextual information. However, a bounding box is not an object mask and usually contains background pixels, so blurring the box does not guarantee that only foreground pixels are modified or that all context remains intact. The implementation is also underspecified: the paper does not state the kernel size or truncation radius for each sigma, padding and boundary handling, whether pixels outside the box contribute to the convolution, whether rectangular boxes or segmentation masks are used, or how overlapping/multiple boxes are handled. These choices affect both reproducibility and the central interpretation that object evidence is perturbed while background evidence is preserved.

comments.title: Preprocessing normalization is ambiguous and possibly inconsistent with pretrained feature extractors
comments.quote: All images from have been resized and cropped to 224x224 and normalized with CIFAR-10 mean and standard deviation.
comments.explanation: Ignoring the apparent omission after “from,” the preprocessing description is ambiguous. If all images from both MS COCO and CIFAR-10 were normalized using CIFAR-10 statistics, that is unusual for ImageNet- and Places365-pretrained ResNet18 feature extractors, which are normally paired with the normalization statistics used during pretraining. If the sentence refers only to CIFAR-10, then the MS COCO preprocessing remains unspecified. This matters because the paper relies on frozen or partly frozen pretrained feature extractors whose representations can be sensitive to input normalization.

comments.title: FGSM implementation is underspecified for reproducibility and interpretation of epsilon
comments.quote: Here, small amounts of noise were added to the test images based on the gradient of the loss function with respect to the input. ... The magnitude of the added noise was quantified by ϵ.
comments.explanation: The paper gives the standard FGSM formula, but key implementation choices are missing. It does not specify whether epsilon is measured in raw pixel space or normalized input space, what pixel scale is used, whether adversarial images are clipped back to the valid image range, whether the attack is targeted or untargeted, whether true or predicted labels are used, or the exact epsilon values used in the main robustness curves. These details can substantially change attack strength, especially when images are resized and normalized before being fed into pretrained networks.

comments.title: Gradient-based robustness claims rely on a non-adaptive foreground-network attack
comments.quote: Adversarial images were created from the full original images using FGSM method Goodfellow et al. (2014) applied to the foreground network, and performance was tested with the foreground, background and joint networks. ... These results suggest that ... a principled way to combine contextual information can be useful for adversarial robustness.
comments.explanation: Generating FGSM examples on the foreground classifier and evaluating them on the background and joint classifiers is a meaningful transfer or partial-channel experiment. It tests whether perturbations crafted for the object-centric stream leave useful contextual information available. However, it does not evaluate an adaptive white-box adversary against the fused model itself. A standard robustness evaluation would attack the defended joint classifier directly, often with stronger iterative attacks such as PGD, because weak transfer or gradient masking can otherwise be mistaken for robustness. The evidence therefore supports robustness to foreground-targeted transferred FGSM examples, not general gradient-based adversarial robustness of the fusion model.

comments.title: Final-layer weight magnitudes alone do not establish stream contribution
comments.quote: To reveal relative contribution of two networks (object-centric and scene-centric) to the joint classifier, we examined the weights of the trained joint classifier ... and found that the foreground and background networks influenced the joint decision almost equally. ... We further examined the weights of the joint classifier after training ... which revealed that the foreground features dominated the joint decision.
comments.explanation: Inspecting final-layer weights is a reasonable qualitative diagnostic, but average absolute weight magnitudes do not by themselves determine how much each stream contributes to the logits. That inference requires the foreground and background feature vectors to have comparable activation scales, variances, sparsity, and normalization. Because the two streams come from different pretrained networks and may have different feature distributions, a branch with smaller weights could still dominate if its activations are larger or more variable, and vice versa. Stronger evidence would come from logit contribution analysis, activation-weight products, feature normalization statistics, stream ablations, gradient attributions, or masking experiments.

comments.title: PCA visualization is overinterpreted as evidence about the full feature subspace
comments.quote: However, for background features, blurred images were represented in the same statistical subspace as clean images.
comments.explanation: The PCA plot can qualitatively illustrate that blurring the object region appears to perturb object-centric features more than scene-centric features. However, a 2D PCA projection of selected classes cannot establish that blurred and clean background features occupy the same statistical subspace in the original high-dimensional feature space. High-dimensional shifts may be hidden by the projection. The claim should be framed more cautiously unless supported by quantitative distances, covariance comparisons, classification tests, or subspace-angle analyses in the full feature space.

comments.title: Clean-accuracy tradeoff claim is stronger than the shown comparison warrants
comments.quote: Importantly, joint classifier revealed the same level of performance as the foreground one for intact images, suggesting that our approach can overcome a common problem of the adversarial defense - degraded performance for intact images.
comments.explanation: It is useful that the joint classifier does not visibly reduce clean accuracy relative to the foreground classifier in this experiment. However, the broader claim that the approach can overcome a common problem of adversarial defense is not established by this comparison alone. The experiment does not compare against well-tuned adversarially trained models, does not report uncertainty or statistical testing, and evaluates a transferred FGSM setting rather than a full adaptive robustness benchmark. A safer conclusion is that, in this setup, adding the background stream did not visibly reduce clean accuracy relative to the foreground baseline.

comments.title: Adversarial-retraining baseline is underspecified and may not be a like-for-like comparison
comments.quote: We also compared the performance of the regularized joint network with a standard method to combat FGSM attacks, adversarial retraining Madry et al. (2017). ... the joint network with higher regulation outperformed the adversarial retraining strategy at all levels of ϵ.
comments.explanation: The baseline comparison lacks enough detail to interpret the claimed advantage. Madry et al. is normally associated with multi-step PGD adversarial training for a specified threat model, not simply a generic method for FGSM attacks. The paper does not specify whether retraining used FGSM or PGD, the number of attack steps, step size, projection radius, training epsilon, clean/adversarial mixing ratio, which model was retrained, or whether the same frozen-feature constraints were imposed. It is also unclear whether the regularized joint model and the adversarially retrained baseline are evaluated under equivalent threat models. If the regularized model benefits from shifting weight to a background stream that the foreground-generated FGSM attack does not target, while the baseline is trained or evaluated differently, then the result does not establish a general advantage over adversarial retraining.

comments.title: Foreground-only L2 regularization cannot symmetrically bias toward either stream
comments.quote: This allowed the network to bias the classifier decision towards the background or the foreground based on the value of α.
comments.explanation: The described mechanism applies an L2 penalty specifically to the foreground weights. Increasing alpha can discourage reliance on the foreground stream and thereby push the classifier toward background/contextual features. But a nonnegative penalty on only the foreground weights does not symmetrically bias the model toward either stream: setting alpha small or zero merely removes the extra foreground penalty and lets the data determine the solution. If the intended meaning is a continuum from an unregularized, often foreground-dominated classifier to a background-biased classifier, that should be stated more precisely.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an interesting and potentially useful idea—improving adversarial robustness by fusing object- and scene/context-oriented representations—but the current presentation raises substantial concerns about clarity, rigor, and positioning. The biological motivation is engaging but overextended and sometimes speculative, and the method is not yet clearly distinguished from standard ensemble, feature-fusion, or context-bias approaches; in particular, calling the approach “multimodal” is somewhat misleading if both streams are visual CNN features from the same image rather than genuinely different modalities. The introduction would benefit from a sharper statement of the technical contribution, the exact fusion mechanism, and why it should improve robustness beyond ensembling or exploiting dataset/context correlations. The adversarial robustness framing also needs much more precision: the paper should define the threat model, attack settings, white-box vs. black-box assumptions, whether attacks are run through the full fused model, and how it avoids common pitfalls such as gradient masking or robustness from non-differentiable preprocessing. Claims that existing defenses “mostly” fail or that biological fusion confers adversarial stability are too broad without careful qualification. The writing contains numerous grammatical, formatting, and citation issues—e.g., “robust ness,” missing spaces before citations, encoding errors, inconsistent terminology—which reduce confidence in the paper’s polish. Overall, the core hypothesis is promising, especially if the experiments show clean-accuracy-preserving gains under strong adaptive attacks, but the paper needs a clearer methodological exposition, a more disciplined related-work comparison, and substantially more rigorous adversarial evaluation to support its main claims.

comments.title: Gaussian blur is treated as an adversarial attack without an adversarial construction
comments.quote: We tested the benefits of the fusion approach on preserving adversarial robustness for human perceivable (e.g., Gaussian blur) and network perceivable (e.g., gradient-based) attacks for CIFAR-10 and MS COCO data sets.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as the authors distinguishing two threat models: perceptible corruptions and imperceptible gradient-based adversarial examples. The later methodology confirms that the gradient-based examples are generated with FGSM, but Gaussian blur is applied by convolving foreground pixels within the object bounding box with a Gaussian kernel at chosen standard deviations. That is a meaningful robustness test, and the foreground-only blur is a reasonable way to probe whether contextual features help. What remains problematic is the terminology: in standard adversarial-robustness usage, Gaussian blur is usually a common corruption or distribution shift unless its location, strength, or parameters are selected by an adversary to maximize loss under a specified constraint. Calling it a “human perceivable ... attack” may lead readers to interpret the blur results as adversarial robustness results in the same sense as FGSM/PGD, which the described procedure does not establish.

comments.title: Central “multimodal” framing is stronger than the actual visual feature-fusion setup
comments.quote: We propose that this biologically inspired approach to integrate information across multiple modalities provides a new way to improve adversarial robustness that can be complementary to current state of the art approaches.
comments.explanation: The biological motivation discusses integration across sensory modalities such as vision, audition, and olfaction, so at first I expected the method to fuse genuinely different input modalities. Checking the paper context, however, the implemented system feeds the same RGB images through two ResNet18 feature extractors pretrained on different visual datasets, Places365 and ImageNet, and concatenates the resulting background/scene-oriented and foreground/object-oriented visual representations. That is a legitimate and potentially useful multi-stream or multi-feature fusion method, and the authors are clear elsewhere that they use background and foreground features. The remaining concern is that describing the method as integration “across multiple modalities” overstates what is demonstrated: the inputs are not separate sensory modalities, and the robustness gain may come from different pretrained visual biases rather than multimodal integration in the usual machine-learning sense.

comments.title: Overbroad characterization of existing adversarial defenses
comments.quote: While many methods exist for detecting and defending against adversarial attacks, they do not generalise across a range of attacks and negatively affect performance on clean, unperturbed data.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended motivation: many proposed defenses are attack-specific, and adversarial training often involves a clean-accuracy/robust-accuracy tradeoff. That is a standard and important concern in adversarial ML. However, the sentence is phrased categorically, as though existing detection and defense methods generally both fail to generalize and necessarily hurt clean accuracy. The later introduction itself mentions stronger adversarial-training variants, and by 2022 there were well-established defenses that are effective within specified threat models, even if they do not solve robustness universally. A more careful formulation such as “many defenses can be attack-specific and may reduce clean accuracy” would be better supported. As written, the claim mildly overstates the literature and could make the paper’s contribution appear stronger than the evidence warrants.

comments.title: Claim that training-phase robustness methods are “mostly ineffective” is not adequately qualified
comments.quote: Although it has been proposed that adversarial and manipulation robustness can be increased through various mechanisms during the training phase, recent research has shown that these methods are mostly ineffective or their effectiveness is inconclusive Uesato et al. (2018) Geirhos et al. (2018); Athalye et al. (2018).
comments.explanation: The authors are right that many proposed defenses have failed under stronger evaluation, and Uesato et al. and Athalye et al. are relevant references for pitfalls such as gradient masking and inadequate attack evaluation. Still, after checking the surrounding context, this sentence seems to sweep too broadly. Those works do not imply that training-phase robustness methods as a class are mostly ineffective; for example, PGD-style adversarial training is widely accepted as effective against the specific norm-bounded white-box threat models it is trained for, albeit with limitations and tradeoffs. Geirhos et al. is also more about texture/shape bias and generalization under corruptions than a direct demonstration that training-phase adversarial defenses are mostly ineffective. The point would be sounder if narrowed to “many proposed defenses have later been shown ineffective under stronger evaluation, and robustness gains can be threat-model specific.”

comments.title: Fine-tuning description conflicts with the later CIFAR-10 experimental setup
comments.quote: Only the 512 dimensional fully connected layer was finetuned for MS COCO Lin et al. (2015) or CIFAR-10 Krizhevsky & Hinton (2009).
comments.explanation: At first this reads as a clear methodological choice: both MS COCO and CIFAR-10 use frozen pretrained ResNet18 feature extractors and only a final 512-dimensional classifier layer is fine-tuned. That would be a reasonable late-fusion setup. However, later in the results section the authors state that, for CIFAR-10, the foreground classifier was trained in all layers from ImageNet initialization, while the background classifier had only its final layer trained. That later detail does not resolve the concern; it directly contradicts this general methodology statement. The distinction matters because adversarial transfer, clean accuracy, and the relative dominance of foreground versus background features depend strongly on whether the feature extractor is frozen or fully fine-tuned. The paper should separate the MS COCO and CIFAR-10 training protocols explicitly.

comments.title: MS COCO dataset size is numerically misstated
comments.quote: MS COCO dataset is a large dataset with 1.6 million images that have multiple objects and multiple instances with overlapping contexts.
comments.explanation: I initially interpreted this as a high-level description of COCO and checked whether the number might refer to annotations rather than images. Standard MS COCO has on the order of a few hundred thousand images, not 1.6 million images; approximately 1.5 million is closer to the number of labeled object instances/annotations. This is likely a wording error rather than a methodological flaw, especially since the authors later report their final pruned subset size. Still, as written it is a numerical inconsistency that could mislead readers about the scale of the source dataset.

comments.title: COCO pruning criterion is internally ambiguous
comments.quote: We pruned the MS COCO dataset for images with fewer than two instances (<=2) of a single object so as to minimise cases of co-occurring context.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intent seems to be to reduce confounding from multiple co-occurring objects and contexts, which is appropriate for testing whether background context helps classification. But the criterion is hard to reconstruct: 'fewer than two instances' would normally mean 0 or 1 instance, whereas the parenthetical '<=2' means at most two instances. It is also unclear whether 'of a single object' means a single object category, a single object instance, or images containing only one annotated category possibly with multiple instances. This ambiguity is not just stylistic, because the resulting COCO subset and the amount of remaining contextual confounding depend directly on this filtering rule.

comments.title: Bounding-box blur is described as leaving context intact, but bounding boxes include background pixels
comments.quote: In our approach, only the foreground pixels are modified leaving the context of the images intact.
comments.explanation: The broader method makes clear that the authors use object bounding boxes to define the region to blur, and they even define foreground earlier as the area within the bounding box. That explains their intended operational definition. However, a bounding box is not an object mask: it usually contains substantial surrounding background/context pixels, especially for irregularly shaped objects or loose annotations. Therefore, saying that only foreground pixels are modified and context is left intact overstates what the procedure guarantees. This matters for interpreting the key claim that foreground/object features are perturbed while background/context features remain unaffected; some contextual evidence inside the bounding box may also be blurred.

comments.title: Gaussian blur procedure lacks enough implementation detail for reproduction
comments.quote: Gaussian blur was applied to the test set by convolving a portion of the image within the object bounding box given in the dataset with Gaussian kernel. Differing degrees of blur were created by varying the standard deviation of the kernel.
comments.explanation: The general idea is clear: blur the object region while leaving the rest of the image unchanged. But for reproducing the perturbation, several important choices are unspecified. The kernel size or truncation radius for each \(\sigma\), padding/boundary handling at the bounding-box edge, whether the blur is computed using pixels only inside the box or using neighboring pixels outside the box, and how overlapping or multiple boxes are handled all affect the resulting image. These details are especially important for large reported blur levels such as \(\sigma=45\), where kernel support and boundary treatment can substantially change the perturbation.

comments.title: Preprocessing normalization is ambiguous and possibly inconsistent with pretrained feature extractors
comments.quote: All images from have been resized and cropped to 224x224 and normalized with CIFAR-10 mean and standard deviation.
comments.explanation: Ignoring the apparent OCR omission after 'from', the substantive statement is ambiguous. If it means all images from both MS COCO and CIFAR-10 were normalized using CIFAR-10 statistics, that is unusual for ImageNet- and Places365-pretrained ResNet18 feature extractors, which are normally used with the normalization statistics from their pretraining regime. If it only refers to CIFAR-10 images, then the preprocessing for MS COCO is left unspecified. This matters because the paper relies on frozen or partly frozen pretrained foreground/background feature extractors, and input normalization can materially affect their representations and robustness.

comments.title: FGSM attack implementation is under-specified for a robustness comparison
comments.quote:
Here, small amounts of noise were added to the test images based on the gradient of the loss function with respect to the input. Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation

where _ϵ_ is a small real number, _J_ is the loss as a function of the parameters ( _θ_ ), the input ( _x_ ), and the label _y_ .
comments.explanation: The authors correctly point to the standard FGSM construction, and the later results clarify that adversarial examples were crafted on the foreground network. However, for reproducing and interpreting the robustness curves, the description still leaves out key attack details: whether \(\epsilon\) is measured in raw pixel space or normalized input space, whether adversarial examples are clipped to the valid image range after perturbation, whether the attack is untargeted or targeted, and what exact \(\epsilon\) values are used. These choices can substantially affect reported robustness, particularly when comparing a frozen-background branch and a fine-tuned foreground branch.

comments.title: Statement about multi-step adversarial training being 'initially proposed' by Madry is overstated
comments.quote: Consequently, adversarial training with multi step attack is regarded as the state of the art method used for improving adversarial robustness for white box and black box attacks and was initially proposed in Madry et al. (2017).
comments.explanation: The authors are right that PGD-style multi-step adversarial training from Madry et al. became a central and very strong baseline for empirical robustness, especially against first-order white-box adversaries. The concern is with the wording. Adversarial training itself predates Madry et al., and even the use of stronger iterative attacks as an evaluation/defense component has related predecessors; Madry et al. is more accurately described as popularizing/formalizing the robust-optimization PGD adversarial-training framework. The claim that it was 'initially proposed' there is therefore historically too strong. The statement also compresses white-box and black-box robustness together, whereas PGD adversarial training is primarily motivated and evaluated as a white-box first-order defense, with black-box robustness being a related but not identical guarantee.

comments.title: FGSM attack is non-adaptive to the joint model, so robustness claims are limited
comments.quote: Adversarial images were created from the full original images using FGSM method Goodfellow et al. (2014) applied to the foreground network, and performance was tested with the foreground, background and joint networks.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as an evaluation of the fused classifier against gradient-based adversarial attacks. The sentence clarifies, however, that the FGSM examples are generated using only the foreground network and then evaluated on the background and joint classifiers. That is a legitimate transferred-attack/black-box-style experiment for the joint model, and it is useful for testing whether perturbations crafted for the object stream transfer to the fused representation. What remains problematic is that the surrounding language asks how well fusion can “defend” against gradient-based attacks, but this setup does not test an adaptive white-box adversary against the joint classifier. Standard robustness evaluation would also attack the fused model directly, and often with stronger iterative attacks such as PGD, because gradient masking or weak transfer can otherwise be mistaken for robustness. Thus the evidence supports robustness to foreground-network FGSM transfer, not general gradient-based adversarial robustness of the fusion model.

comments.title: CIFAR-10 training protocol contradicts the earlier stated finetuning protocol
comments.quote: For the foreground classifier, we trained all layers of a Resnet18 on CIFAR-10, with weights initialized from a Resnet 18 pretrained on Imagenet.
comments.explanation: This caught my attention because the methodology earlier states that only the 512-dimensional final fully connected layer was finetuned for MS COCO or CIFAR-10, while the joint model finetunes the concatenated representation. The passage here gives a different protocol for the CIFAR-10 foreground classifier: all layers are trained. This may be an intentional exception motivated by CIFAR-10 being object-centric and larger, and the authors do explain a special treatment for the background classifier to preserve scene-centric features. But as written, it conflicts with the earlier general methodological description. The distinction matters because full finetuning changes the feature distribution, clean accuracy, adversarial vulnerability, and fairness of comparison with the background and joint models.

comments.title: FGSM implementation details are underspecified for reproducibility and interpretation of \(\epsilon\)
comments.quote: The magnitude of the added noise was quantified by _ϵ_ (see Methodology and Goodfellow et al. (2014)).
comments.explanation: The paper correctly identifies \(\epsilon\) as the perturbation magnitude in FGSM, and the earlier formula gives the standard sign-gradient update. However, for image attacks the numerical meaning of \(\epsilon\) depends critically on preprocessing: whether the perturbation is applied in raw pixel space or normalized tensor space, whether inputs are clipped back to a valid range, what pixel scale is used, and whether true labels or predicted labels are used in the loss. These choices can substantially change the strength of the attack, especially because the paper resizes and normalizes images before classification. Referring to Goodfellow et al. is not enough to reproduce the reported curves or compare their \(\epsilon\) values with standard CIFAR-10 robustness baselines.

comments.title: Last-layer weight magnitudes are not sufficient to infer equal contribution of foreground and background streams
comments.quote: To reveal relative contribution of two networks (object-centric and scene-centric) to the joint classifier, we examined the weights of the trained joint classifier (Figure 4b) and found that the foreground and background networks influenced the joint decision almost equally.
comments.explanation: It is reasonable to inspect the joint classifier’s final-layer weights as a first qualitative diagnostic, and the authors appropriately present the conclusion as qualitative in the next sentence. Still, average absolute weights alone do not determine the influence of each feature stream unless the foreground and background feature vectors have comparable scales, variances, sparsity, and normalization. A branch with smaller weights but larger activations, or vice versa, can dominate the logits. A more direct analysis would examine logit contributions, activation-weight products, ablations of each stream, gradient attributions, or feature normalization. As written, the inference from similar weight magnitudes to equal influence is not fully justified.

comments.title: PCA visualization is overinterpreted as evidence about the full feature subspace
comments.quote: However, for background features, blurred images were represented in the same statistical subspace as clean images.
comments.explanation: The authors are using PCA visualization to illustrate an intuitive and plausible point: blurring only the object region should perturb object-centric features more than scene-centric features. The figure can support this as a qualitative observation. But the claim that blurred background features remain in the “same statistical subspace” is stronger than what a 2D PCA projection of a few representative classes can establish. A 2D projection can hide high-dimensional shifts, and no quantitative distance, covariance, classification, or subspace-angle analysis is described here. The result should be framed more cautiously unless supported by a quantitative analysis of the original feature space.

comments.title: Gaussian blur procedure leaves important implementation choices implicit
comments.quote: The blur was applied to the bounding box area of the image, where main object was located.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is clear: perturb the foreground while leaving context intact, which directly tests the proposed background/foreground complementarity. But the exact operation is underspecified in ways that affect reproducibility and potentially the results. MS COCO images may contain up to two instances under the paper’s pruning rule, so it is unclear whether all object bounding boxes, one selected box, their union, or a “main” box is blurred. It is also not specified how the Gaussian kernel size is chosen for a given \(\sigma\), how boundaries at the box edge are handled, whether blur mixes pixels from outside the bounding box, and whether masks are rectangular boxes or object segmentations. Since the claimed advantage depends on preserving background while corrupting only foreground, these boundary and masking choices are methodologically important.

comments.title: Similar/Dissimilar subset construction is too underspecified to support the context-variability conclusion
comments.quote: ‘Dissimilar’ and ‘Similar’ are eight classes randomly selected from the 24 classes such that the supercategories are either distinct or the same (see Methodology).
comments.explanation: The authors’ intent is understandable: using COCO supercategories is a sensible way to create conditions where context is either more or less discriminative. The concern is that the results appear to depend on one random selection of eight classes, but the passage does not identify the classes, the random seed, or whether results were averaged over multiple random draws. Different COCO categories can vary substantially in object size, background regularity, dataset bias, and baseline difficulty, so a single selected subset could produce an apparent “context variability” effect for reasons unrelated to the Similar/Dissimilar manipulation. The conclusion would be better supported by reporting the sampled classes and/or repeating the subset sampling with variance estimates.

comments.title: Clean-accuracy tradeoff claim is stronger than the shown comparison warrants
comments.quote: Importantly, joint classifier revealed the same level of performance as the foreground one for intact images, suggesting that our approach can overcome a common problem of the adversarial defense - degraded performance for intact images (Figure 4a).
comments.explanation: It is a useful observation that the fused classifier apparently does not lose clean accuracy relative to the foreground classifier on this MS COCO setup. That is relevant because adversarial training often trades off clean and robust accuracy. However, the passage moves from this single comparison to the broader statement that the approach can overcome a common problem of adversarial defense. The experiment does not compare against adversarially trained models, does not report uncertainty or statistical testing, and evaluates a transferred FGSM setting rather than a fully adaptive robustness benchmark. A more precise claim would be that, in this experiment, adding the background stream did not visibly reduce clean accuracy relative to the foreground baseline.

comments.title: Robustness claim is based on a non-adaptive foreground-network attack
comments.quote: These results suggest that although adversarial examples do transfer across classifiers, a principled way to combine contextual information can be useful for adversarial robustness without the added cost of adversarial retraining and compromising accuracy on unperturbed data.
comments.explanation: The empirical setup is clear earlier in the paper: FGSM examples are generated using the foreground classifier and then evaluated on the foreground, background, and joint classifiers. That is a meaningful transfer/partial-channel experiment, and the authors are right that it tests whether an attack aimed at the object-centric stream leaves useful contextual information intact. However, the quoted sentence reads more broadly as a claim about adversarial robustness in general. Under the standard adversarial-robustness convention, especially after Athalye et al. and Madry et al., a defense should be evaluated against an adaptive white-box attacker that attacks the defended model itself. Here, an attacker aware of the fused model could optimize the loss of the joint classifier, or specifically exploit the background stream. The result therefore supports robustness to foreground-targeted or transferred FGSM attacks, not necessarily robustness to adaptive attacks on the fusion model. The sentence would be safer if it explicitly qualified the threat model.

comments.title: Adversarial-retraining baseline is underspecified and may not match the cited standard
comments.quote: We also compared the performance of the regularized joint network with a standard method to combat FGSM attacks, adversarial retraining Madry et al. (2017).
comments.explanation: The intent is understandable: the authors want to compare their foreground-regularized fusion strategy against an established adversarial-training defense. But the description does not provide enough methodological detail to interpret the comparison. Madry et al. is normally associated with multi-step PGD adversarial training for an \(\ell_\infty\) threat model, not simply a generic 'standard method to combat FGSM attacks.' The passage and Figure 5 caption mention adversarial examples generated at \(\epsilon = 0.3\), but do not specify whether the retraining used FGSM, PGD, number of steps, step size, projection radius, clean/adversarial mixing ratio, which model was retrained, or whether the same frozen-feature constraints were imposed. Since adversarial-training strength is highly sensitive to these choices, the later claim that regularization outperforms adversarial retraining could reflect a weak or mismatched baseline rather than an intrinsic advantage of the method.

comments.title: Final-layer weight magnitudes alone do not establish stream contribution
comments.quote: We further examined the weights of the joint classifier after training (Figure 4d) which revealed that the foreground features dominated the joint decision.
comments.explanation: It is reasonable to inspect the final-layer weights as a first diagnostic, and the figure caption makes clear that the plotted quantity is the average absolute value of foreground and background weights. However, interpreting those averages as dominance in the decision requires an additional assumption: that foreground and background feature activations are on comparable scales and have comparable variances/distributions. Since the two streams come from different pretrained networks, and potentially different feature normalizations, a smaller average weight on one stream could still have a large effect if its activations are larger or more variable, and vice versa. This concern also affects the earlier MS COCO interpretation that approximately equal average weights imply approximately equal influence. A more robust analysis would report normalized feature statistics, contribution magnitudes such as \(|w_j x_j|\), ablations of each stream, or performance after stream masking.

comments.title: Foreground-only L2 regularization cannot symmetrically bias toward either stream
comments.quote: This allowed the network to bias the classifier decision towards the background or the foreground based on the value of _α_ .
comments.explanation: The described mechanism is an L2 penalty specifically on the foreground weights \(\theta_{fg}\). That makes sense for the stated known-adversary setting: increasing \(\alpha\) should discourage reliance on the attacked foreground stream and push the classifier toward background/contextual features. But as written, the claim is symmetric: it says the value of \(\alpha\) can bias the classifier toward either background or foreground. With a nonnegative L2 coefficient on only the foreground weights, increasing \(\alpha\) can only penalize foreground reliance; setting \(\alpha\) small or zero merely removes that extra penalty and lets the classifier choose based on the data. It does not actively regularize toward the foreground in the same way. If the intended meaning is a continuum from an unregularized, often foreground-dominated solution to a background-biased solution, that should be stated more precisely.

comments.title: Comparison to adversarial retraining is not clearly under the same threat model
comments.quote: Surprisingly, the joint network with higher regulation outperformed the adversarial retraining strategy at all levels of _ϵ_ .
comments.explanation: The result may be valid for the particular experiment shown, especially because the authors explicitly assume a known adversary targeting the foreground stream. What remains unclear is whether the adversarially retrained baseline and the regularized joint model are being evaluated under equivalent conditions. The regularized model appears to benefit from shifting weight to a background stream that the foreground-generated FGSM attack does not directly target, whereas a standard adversarial-training baseline would usually be evaluated against attacks crafted on the model being defended. If adversarial retraining was applied only to the foreground classifier, or trained at a single \(\epsilon\), or evaluated against transferred rather than adaptive attacks, then the comparison does not establish that foreground regularization is generally stronger than adversarial retraining. The claim should be qualified to the foreground-targeted FGSM setting unless an adaptive, like-for-like baseline is provided.
